CITY OF CARLSBAD
1200 CARLSBAD VILLAGE DRIVE
CARLSBAD, CALIFORNIA 92008-1989
(619) 434-2891

VINCENT F. BIONDO, JR.
CITY ATTORNEY

RONALD F. BALL
ASSISTANT CITY ATTORNEY

April 17, 1990


Deanne Stone
Kathy Donovan
Fair Political Practices Commission
Enforcement Division
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

RE:    REQUEST FOR FPPC OPINION - COUNCIL MEMBER MAMAUX'S
       PARTICIPATION IN DECISIONS REGARDING ESTABLISHMENT OF A
       CITY-WIDE COMMUNITY FACILITIES DISTRICT

Ladies:

     This is in response to a letter dated April 3, 1990 from Tom Smith.

     The City contemplates the issuance of $117 million of "Mello-Roos"
bonds to finance facilities required by the City's voter approved,
award winning growth management program.  Tom Smith has
consistently opposed growth management.  See Concerned Citizens v.
Carlsbad, (1988) 204 Cal.App.3d 937.

     The only interest bond counsel or I have in this matter is to
insure the integrity of the bond issue.  Bond counsel will be
issuing a legal opinion in that regard.  The absurdity of Smith's
suggestion that bond counsel or I would compromise ourselves or the
bond issue by giving you anything else but the true and correct
facts in our March 21, 1990 request for advice speaks for itself.
We are used to Smith's attacks on people's character which
characterize his approach to politics and they require no reply.

     F. MacKenzie Brown has acted as the City's bond counsel for
assessment district proceedings in Carlsbad for over 18 years.  One
of Smith's complaints involves Mr. Mamaux's participation in the
Palomar Airport Road Assessment District.  As previously reported
bond counsel has given an opinion on that which does not relate in
any way to the request for advice we have sent you on the proposed
city-wide Community Facilities District.

     I have sent a copy of Smith's letter to bond counsel with a request
that, to the extent any reply is necessary for a full disclosure
of the facts, they communicate directly with you.  I have also sent
a copy to Mr. Mamaux in case he thinks it is necessary to comment.

I can see this is a scanned document image, but the text is too faint and unclear for me to transcribe accurately. The image appears to be heavily faded or low contrast, making the content illegible. There are some watermarks visible (including what appears to be a seal or emblem in the upper left), and there seem to be multiple sections or pages, but I cannot make out the actual text with sufficient confidence to transcribe it verbatim as requested.

To provide an accurate transcription following your rules, I would need a clearer, higher-contrast version of this document.

We object to Smith's suggestion that the City's request for advice
be delayed pending a resolution of his complaints against Mr.
Mamaux. There is no rational connection. Staff believes the
Community Facilities District is necessary to finance the public
facilities required to preserve the quality of life in Carlsbad.
They intend to submit the first step in the formation process, a
resolution of intention, to the Council in the next few weeks.
We ask that you not encourage Mr. Smith in his efforts to oppose
that by responding to his request for a delay. A prompt response
to our request for advice and the resolution of all of the pending
complaints against Mr. Mamaux as soon as possible will be in the
best interests of all concerned. Please let me know if there is
anything I can do to help.

Very truly yours,

VINCENT F. BIONDO, JR.
City Attorney

C: John Mamaux
F. MacKenzie Brown
John Wallace

I can see this image appears to be a scanned document, but the text is too faint or low contrast for me to reliably read and transcribe the specific content accurately. The image shows what appears to be a multi-page document with three sections separated by fold lines, but the text throughout is not legible enough for me to provide a verbatim transcription while adhering to your requirement of exact accuracy.

To provide you with an accurate transcription following your rules, I would need a clearer or higher contrast version of this document.

CITY OF CARLSBAD
1200 CARLSBAD VILLAGE DRIVE
CARLSBAD, CALIFORNIA 92008-1989
(619) 434-2891

VINCENT F. BIONDO, JR.
CITY ATTORNEY

RONALD F. BALL
ASSISTANT CITY ATTORNEY

March 21, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

RE: REQUEST FOR FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION OPINION
COUNCIL MEMBER MAMAUX'S PARTICIPATION IN DECISIONS REGARDING
ESTABLISHMENT OF A CITY-WIDE COMMUNITY FACILITIES DISTRICT

The City of Carlsbad as one means of implementing its award winning
growth management program contemplates the formation of a
$117,000,000 city-wide community facilities district under the
provisions of the Mello-Roos Community Facilities Act of 1982. The
bond issue proceeds will be used for public facilities including
a library, city hall, regional park, major arterial roads and
freeway interchanges. The program does not relieve developers of
any responsibility but allows the City to "finance the developer
fees" and construct the public facilities required by growth
management. Mr. Mamaux's employer owns approximately three percent
of the land which would be subject to the levy of a special tax if
the district is established.

Enclosed is a copy of a "Request for Opinion of the Fair Political
Practices Commission" prepared by the City's bond counsel giving
the facts which we ask the Commission to consider in reaching its
opinion. Also enclosed is a copy of our growth management
ordinance and detailed information on the proposed district.

Please let me know if you need any further information in order to
expedite your response to this request.

Very truly yours,

VINCENT F. BIONDO, JR.
City Attorney

rmh
enclosures

c: Mayor and Council
City Manager
Warren Diven, Bond Counsel

CITY OF CARLSBAD
1200 CARLSBAD VILLAGE DRIVE
CARLSBAD, CALIFORNIA 92008-1989
(619) 434-2891

VINCENT F. BIONDO, JR.
CITY ATTORNEY

RONALD R. BALL
ASSISTANT CITY ATTORNEY

August 14, 1990

John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

RE: REQUEST FOR INFORMAL ASSISTANCE
YOUR FILE NO. I-90-241

Dear Mr. Wallace:

Thank you for your letter of August 1, 1990 regarding the above referenced assistance request.

It remains the opinion of bond counsel and my opinion that the city wide Mello Roos district adopted to implement our voter approved growth management system does not implicate the conflict of interest laws regarding Mr. Mamaux. This letter is to request that you please take another look at the matter and, in particular, specifically address our belief that the public generally exception applies. In that regard, enclosed is a map of the Mello Roos district boundaries prepared by our Finance Director. All the undeveloped land in the pink and yellow areas to be included in the district must constitute the "general public."

I was concerned by the statement in your letter that you lacked information concerning the number of property owners in the district. Enclosed, for your ready reference, is my original request letter dated March 21, 1990 which included a copy of a "request for opinion" prepared by bond counsel which was a two page document giving all of the pertinent facts regarding the Mello as it was then proposed. I am unclear what additional facts you require and it occurs to me that, perhaps, our original opinion request was mislaid.

Subsequent to our original request, substantial additional work has been done by our Finance Director and financial consultants on the structure of the Mello Roos district. The Finance Director has prepared some additional up to date materials which we ask that you consider in reaching your opinion. First, is a memorandum dated

2

August 10, 1990 giving the overall information.  Phase I of the
Mello will comprise 10,261 acres and 185 owners.  Ultimately, the
district will cover 15,245 acres and 420 owners.  Phase I will
encompass approximately 40% of all the land in the City and
ultimately the district will cover 60%. The city of Carlsbad is 39
square miles or about 25,400 acres.  The maximum property owner
controlled by the principals that are a source of income to Council
Member Mamaux are approximately 2.2% of the total Mello area. The
tax for industrial and commercial property will be passed on to the
purchasers of the lots as they are developed.  There will
ultimately be many thousands of tax payers effected by this
district. All residential assessments must either be paid at the
option of the homeowner or included in the loan.  Carlsbad's
current population is approximately 65,000 and at buildout we will
more then double. There will be ultimately be more people effected
by the district then are presently living in Carlsbad.  Taking all
those facts together, we think you should conclude that the public
generally exception applies.

The Finance Director has also prepared some backup data.  The
yellow pages sort the mailing for property owners by zone, the blue pages
alphabetically by owner's name, the pink pages by gross acres and
the green page lists the proposed projects. The Finance Director
has a detailed computer list of every individual project and if you
have any questions you should feel free to call him directly at
(619) 434-2867.

I would also like you to take another look at your assumption that
the presence of the Mello will make the property substantially more
valuable. I don't think that's true since whatever value is added
by the addition of the planned facilities will be offset by an
increased tax burden which will be assessed to each parcel.  Stated
differently, although the property may be more valuable because
they have certain facilities available the net effect to the
property owner will be zero because those benefits will be offset
and reduced in an equal amount by the additional tax burdens
imposed by the community facilities district.

You can see from the map that the City has been divided into 25
zones for growth management purposes.  The City Council is
requiring each zone as it comes in with a public facilities plan to
participate in the Mello.  Your opinion means in a practical effect
that Mr. Mamaux may not participate in the adoption of financing
plans for substantially all of the City. It seems to me the public
generally exception exists to prevent that kind of problem from
occurring.

Please give this request your best attention. The matter has been
dragging on for most of this year and we would like to conclude it

3

as soon as possible. If you have any questions or concerns please
give me a call. Your cooperation and courtesy is appreciated.

Very truly yours,

VINCENT F. BIONDO, JR.
City Attorney

rmh
enclosures

c: Council Member Mamaux
Finance Director Elliott
(with enclosures)

REQUEST FOR OPINION OF THE

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

QUESTION

Based upon the facts set forth below, does Councilmember John Mamaux
have a financial interest which would preclude him under the
provisions of Government Code § 87100 from participating in the
decision by the City Council of the City of Carlsbad to form a
citywide community facilities district under the provisions of the
Mello-Roos Community Facilities Act of 1982?

In 1986 the City Council of the City of Carlsbad adopted Chapter 21.90
of the City's Municipal Code establishing a growth management plan for
the City. A copy of Chapter 21.90 is attached for your reference.
Chapter 21.90 required the preparation of a Citywide Facilities and
Improvements Plan which was adopted by the City Council in 1986
("Citywide Plan"). The Citywide plan established numerous
geographical zones within the City and required the establishment of
plans for each zone (collectively, the "Local Plans"). The Citywide
plan and Local Plans create standards for the financing and
construction of a wide variety of public facilities including parks,
roads, freeway intersections, libraries, fire stations, civic center
and other public facilities. These Plans have been adopted by the
City Council. The Plans identify establishment of a community
facilities district as one method for financing the public facilities
and services identified in the various Plans.

Section 21.90.130 provides that no development permit shall be
approved unless such a permit is consistent with the Local Facilities
Management Plan and unless provisions for all facilities and
improvements related to the development project are provided or
funded in a manner acceptable to the City Council.

In order to guarantee the provision and funding of certain facilities
which are required prior to the issuance of development permits within
the City, the formation of a community facilities district has been
proposed. The community facilities district, if formed, would
authorize the issuance of approximately $117,000,000 in special tax
bonds, which would be secured by the levy of special taxes against
undeveloped property within the City of Carlsbad. The bonds would be
utilized to finance all or a portion of a library, City hall
facility, regional park, local roads, freeway interchanges, and a
utility maintenance warehouse, as well as incidental expenditures and
costs of issuance for the bonds. The City has previously determined
that the construction or guarantee of the financing of the
construction of these facilities is a necessary prerequisite under
Chapter 21.90 and the Citywide Plan and various Local Plans to the
issuance of development permits.

As mentioned above, the special tax would be levied at least initially
only against currently undeveloped property. For property designated
for residential development, an annual special tax would be levied for
each developable acre until the issuance of a building permit for that
property. At the time of issuance of a building permit, such
residential property would be subject to a one time special
development tax. Following payment of the special development tax,
the residential property would not be subject to the levy of further
special taxes for this community facilities district, although the
property might be subject to facilities fees established pursuant to
SEction 21.90.050 of Chapter 21.90.

Prior to development, property designated for commercial and
industrial development would pay an annual special tax per developable
acre. Upon the issuance of a building permit for the development of
such property, the owners of such commercial or industrial property
would have the option of paying a one time development tax or assuming
an annual special development tax based upon the square footage of the
building area. A copy of the proposed special tax formula is attached
hereto.

In November, 1989, Councilmember Mamaux became employed by Center
Mortgage, a lending institution, the principal office of which is
located in the City of Carlsbad. The primary focus of the lending
activities of Center Mortgage is to provide financing for $300,000 to
$700,000 commercial and industrial developments. Center Mortgage is
owned by C&N Bank Corp a California corporation the sole shareholder
which is Dean Greenberg. Mr. Greenberg is the owner of Center
Development, a land development company located in the City of
Carlsbad which is the owner of 69 parcels which include approximately
129.66 net taxable acres designated for commercial or industrial use
within Local Facilities Management Zone 5 and 1 parcel which includes
approximately 95.8 net taxable acres within local Facilities
Management Zone 25 of the City. Zone 5 contains a total of 207
taxable parcels which include 910 taxable acres owned by 40 different
property owners. Zone 25 contains a total of 5 taxable parcels which
include 191.52 taxable acres owned by 5 different property owners.
The property owned by Center Development would be subject to the levy
of special taxes if the community facilities district is established.

The Center Development property will also receive the same benefit
received by other undeveloped properties through the City from the
establishment of the community facilities district, the levy of
special taxes and the issuance of bonds, ie., the satisfaction of the
condition precedent to the issuance of development permits by the
establishment of a method for guaranteeing the financing of the
construction of public facilities as required by Chapter 21.90. The
total number of taxable acres within the City, which may be subject to
the levy of special taxes if the community facilities district is
established, is 7393 acres which are located in Zones 4 through 25,
inclusive. The total number of parcels throughout these zones is
1,451 and these parcels are owned by 496 owners. Zones 1 through 3
are not proposed to be included in the community facilities district
because these zones have guaranteed the provision and funding of the
facilities required for these zones through other methods.

CITY OF CARLSBAD

COMMUNITY FACILITIES DISTRICT NO. 1

EXHIBIT "A"

PROPERTY CATEGORIES AND MAXIMUM TAX

All taxable property within the boundaries of the Community
Facilities District shall be subject to ANNUAL TAXES and/or
DEVELOPMENT TAXES as applicable, based upon the property catego-
ries, maximum tax rates, increases and status of the issuance of
building permits, all as hereinafter set forth. Area I refer
to all properties within the boundaries of the City of Carlsbad
Bridge and Thoroughfare District. Area II is for all remaining
properties within the Community Facilities District, not within
the Bridge & Thoroughfare District.

RESIDENTIAL PROPERTY

ANNUAL TAX

All taxable property prior to the issuance of a building permit
shall be subject to the following maximum annual special taxes,
also subject to adjustments as set forth herein:

MAXIMUM ANNUAL SPECIAL TAX RATE
PER DEVELOPABLE ACRE FOR FISCAL
YEAR 1990/91

PROPERTY CATEGORY                    AREA I          AREA II

Residential Low                    $  230.00       $  227.00
Residential Low to Medium             632.00          622.00
Residential Medium                  1,059.00          996.00
Residential Medium to High          1,623.00        1,528.00
Residential High                    2,285.00        2,241.00


SPECIAL DEVELOPMENT TAX

Upon the issuance of a building permit, all Residential proper-
ties shall be subject to the following described ONE TIME
DEVELOPMENT TAX, also subject to adjustments as set forth
herein, generally as follows:

MAXIMUM ONE TIME DEVELOPMENT TAX
PER DEVELOPABLE ACRE FOR FISCAL
YEAR 1990/91

PROPERTY CATEGORY                    AREA I          AREA II

Residential Low                    3,860.00        3,320.00
Residential Low to Medium          3,860.00        3,320.00
Residential Medium                 2,434.00        2,002.00
Residential Medium to High         2,434.00        2,002.00
Residential High                   2,434.00        2,002.00

COMMERCIAL AND INDUSTRIAL PROPERTY

All taxable Commercial and Industrial Property prior to the
issuance of a building permit shall be subject to the following
maximum annual special taxes, also subject to adjustments as set
forth herein:

ANNUAL TAX

MAXIMUM ANNUAL SPECIAL TAX RATE
PER DEVELOPABLE ACRE FOR FISCAL
YEAR 1990/91

AREA I          AREA II

Commercial Property              $ 3,000.00       $ 2,000.00

Industrial Property               1,500.00        1,200.00

Agricultural Property              -0-              -0-

SPECIAL DEVELOPMENT TAX

All Commercial and Industrial Property, upon the issuance of a
building permit, shall have the option of (1) the payment of the
ONE TIME DEVELOPMENT TAX or (2) the assumption of the ANNUAL
SPECIAL DEVELOPMENT TAX for a period of not to exceed twenty-
five (25) years, generally in the amounts per property category
as follows:

MAXIMUM (NOT TO EXCEED) ONE TIME
DEVELOPMENT TAX FOR FISCAL YEAR
1990/91 PER SQUARE FOOT OF BUILD-
ING AREA

ANNUAL TAX INCREASE

The above ANNUAL and DEVELOPMENT TAXES for the taxable properties are applicable for the fiscal year 1990/91, and are all

subject to annual increases to reflect increases in the Construction Cost Index of the Engineering News-Record, Los Angeles, as in effect May 1st of any fiscal year. The maximum annual tax increases for properties is as follows:

A.    PROPERTY WITHIN THE ORIGINAL DISTRICT: Not to exceed 50% of the above-referenced increase.

B.    PROPERTIES TO BE SUBSEQUENTLY ANNEXED: Not to exceed 100% the above-referenced increase until annexed.

Residential:

Once a building permit has been issued for Residential property and the ONE TIME DEVELOPMENT TAX has been paid, the property no longer will be subject to any annual special tax.

Commercial & Industrial:

The Commercial and Industrial properties, upon the issuance of a building permit, have the option to (1) pay the ONE TIME DEVELOPMENT TAX and/or (2) assume the ANNUAL DEVELOPMENT TAX, as set forth above. Upon the issuance of any building permit, no further escalation is authorized.

TERM OF TAX

The above taxes shall be levied until all bonds have been discharged and facilities funded and/or guaranteed, and the ONE TIME DEVELOPMENT TAX has been paid; however, the ANNUAL DEVELOPMENT TAX shall not be levied for a period in excess of twenty-five (25) years.

COLLECTION OF ANNUAL TAX

Residential:

FIRST, determine monies necessary to make payment on bonds, debt service, replenish reserve and pay for public capital facilities.

SECOND, levy and determine amount of funds available from special residential development tax.

THIRD, to the extent monies are necessary, collect the remainder from the annual special tax rate per developable area for undeveloped land.

Commercial and Industrial:

FIRST, determine monies necessary to make payment on bonds, debt service, replenish reserve and pay for public capital facilities.

SECOND, determine monies available from the (a) One Time Special
Development Tax and (b) maximum collected Annual Special Development Tax.

THIRD, to the extent monies are necessary, levy taxes on undeveloped taxable commercial and industrial acreage, not to exceed
the maximum tax.

* * *

sections.question:
Based upon the facts set forth below, does Councilmember John Mamaux have a financial interest which would preclude him under the provisions of Government Code § 87100 from participating in the decision by the City Council of the City of Carlsbad to form a citywide community facilities district under the provisions of the Mello-Roos Community Facilities Act of 1982? In 1986 the City Council of the City of Carlsbad adopted Chapter 21.90 of the City's Municipal Code establishing a growth management plan for the City. A copy of Chapter 21.90 is attached for your reference. Chapter 21.90 required the preparation of a Citywide Facilities and Improvements Plan which was adopted by the City Council in 1986 ("Citywide Plan"). The Citywide plan established numerous geographical zones within the City and required the establishment of plans for each zone (collectively, the "Local Plans"). The Citywide Plan and Local Plans create standards for the financing and construction of a wide variety of public facilities including parks, roads, freeway intersections, libraries, fire stations, civic center and other public facilities. These Plans have been adopted by the City Council. The Plans identify establishment of a community facilities district as one method for financing the public facilities and services identified in the various Plans. Section 21.90.130 provides that no development permit shall be approved unless such a permit is consistent with the Local Facilities Management Plan and unless provisions for all facilities and improvements related to the development project are provided or funded in a manner acceptable to the City Council. In order to guarantee the provision and funding of certain facilities which are required prior to the issuance of development permits within the City, the formation of a community facilities district has been proposed. The community facilities district, if formed, would authorize the issuance of approximately $117,000,000 in special tax bonds, which would be secured by the levy of special taxes against undeveloped property within the City of Carlsbad. The bonds would be utilized to finance all or a portion of a library, City hall facility, regional park, local roads, freeway interchanges, and a utility maintenance warehouse, as well as incidental expenditures and costs of issuance for the bonds. The City has previously determined that the construction or guarantee of the financing of the construction of these facilities is a necessary prerequisite under Chapter 21.90 and the Citywide Plan and various Local Plans to the issuance of development permits. As mentioned above, the special tax would be levied at least initially only against currently undeveloped property. For property designated for residential development, an annual special tax would be levied for each developable acre until the issuance of a building permit for that property. At the time of issuance of a building permit, such residential property would be subject to a one time special development tax. Following payment of the special development tax, the residential property would not be subject to the levy of further special taxes for this community facilities district, although the property might be subject to facilities fees established pursuant to Section 21.90.050 of Chapter 21.90. Prior to development, property designated for commercial and industrial development would pay an annual special tax per developable acre. Upon the issuance of a building permit for the development of such property, the owners of such commercial or industrial property would have the option of paying a one time development tax or assuming an annual special development tax based upon the square footage of the building area. A copy of the proposed special tax formula is attached hereto. In November, 1989, Councilmember Mamaux became employed by Center Mortgage, a lending institution, the principal office of which is located in the City of Carlsbad. The primary focus of the lending activities of Center Mortgage is to provide financing for $300,000 to $700,000 commercial and industrial developments. Center Mortgage is owned by C&N Bank Corp., a California corporation the sole shareholder which is Dean Greenberg. Mr. Greenberg is the owner of Center Development, a land development company located in the City of Carlsbad which is the owner of 69 parcels which include approximately 129.66 net taxable acres designated for commercial or industrial use within Local Facilities Management Zone 5 and 1 parcel which includes approximately 95.8 net taxable acres within Local Facilities Management Zone 25 of the City. Zone 5 contains a total of 207 taxable parcels which include 910 taxable acres owned by 40 different property owners. Zone 25 contains a total of 5 taxable parcels which include 191.52 taxable acres owned by 5 different property owners. The property owned by Center Development would be subject to the levy of special taxes if the community facilities district is established. The Center Development property will also receive the same benefit received by other undeveloped properties throughout the City from the establishment of the community facilities district, the levy of special taxes and the issuance of bonds, ie., the satisfaction of the condition precedent to the issuance of development permits by the establishment of a method for guaranteeing the financing of the construction of public facilities as required by Chapter 21.90. The total number of taxable acres within the City, which may be subject to the levy of special taxes if the community facilities district is established, is 7393 acres which are located in Zones 4 through 25, inclusive. The total number of parcels throughout these zones is 1,451 and these parcels are owned by 496 owners. Zones 1 through 3 are not proposed to be included in the community facilities district because these zones have guaranteed the provision and funding of the facilities required for these zones through other methods. CITY OF CARLSBAD COMMUNITY FACILITIES DISTRICT NO. 1 EXHIBIT "A" PROPERTY CATEGORIES AND MAXIMUM TAX All taxable property within the boundaries of the Community Facilities District shall be subject to ANNUAL TAXES and/or DEVELOPMENT TAXES as applicable, based upon the property categories, maximum tax rates, increases and status of the issuance of building permits, all as hereinafter set forth. Area I refers to all properties within the boundaries of the City of Carlsbad Bridge and Thoroughfare District. Area II is for all remaining properties within the Community Facilities District, not within the Bridge & Thoroughfare District. RESIDENTIAL PROPERTY ANNUAL TAX All taxable property prior to the issuance of a building permit shall be subject to the following maximum annual special taxes, also subject to adjustments as set forth herein: MAXIMUM ANNUAL SPECIAL TAX RATE PER DEVELOPABLE ACRE FOR FISCAL YEAR 1990/91

Fac/Comm. | 0.39 | 0.29 | | Auto - Repair & Sales | 0.39 | 0.15 | | Regional Shopping Center | 0.38 | 0.18 | | Discount Store | 0.37 | 0.18 | | Golf Course | 0.35 | 0.24 | | Church | 0.34 | 0.22 | | Office - Government | 0.33 | 0.21 | | Commercial Shops | 0.33 | 0.17 | | Health Club | 0.33 | 0.21 | | Motel | 0.31 | 0.23 | | Office - High Rise | 0.30 | 0.21 | | Office - Commercial, Less than 100,000 SF | 0.29 | 0.20 | | Lumber/Hardware Store | 0.28 | 0.16 | | Office - Medical | 0.28 | 0.24 | | Hospital - Convalescent | 0.26 | 0.25 | | Indoor Sports Arena | 0.26 | 0.25 | | Resort Hotel | 0.26 | 0.22 | | University | 0.26 | 0.22 | | Junior College | 0.25 | 0.22 | All other Commercial Properties not identified above: 0.24 (Area I), 0.15 (Area II) **INDUSTRIAL USE DESIGNATIONS** | INDUSTRIAL USE DESIGNATIONS | AREA I | AREA II | |----------------------------|--------|--------| | Heliports | 1.21 | 0.42 | | Industrial-Commercial Business Park | 0.24 | 0.17 | | All other Industrial Properties not identified above | 0.20 | 0.15 | **ANNUAL TAX INCREASE** The above ANNUAL and DEVELOPMENT TAXES for the taxable properties are applicable for the fiscal year 1990/91, and are all subject to annual increases to reflect increases in the Construction Cost Index of the Engineering News-Record, Los Angeles, as in effect May 1st of any fiscal year. The maximum annual tax increases for properties is as follows: A. PROPERTY WITHIN THE ORIGINAL DISTRICT: Not to exceed 50% of the above-referenced increase. B. PROPERTIES TO BE SUBSEQUENTLY ANNEXED: Not to exceed 100% the above-referenced increase until annexed. Residential: Once a building permit has been issued for Residential property and the ONE TIME DEVELOPMENT TAX has been paid, the property no longer will be subject to any annual special tax. Commercial & Industrial: The Commercial and Industrial properties, upon the issuance of a building permit, have the option to (1) pay the ONE TIME DEVELOPMENT TAX and/or (2) assume the ANNUAL DEVELOPMENT TAX, as set forth above. Upon the issuance of any building permit, no further escalation is authorized. TERM OF TAX The above taxes shall be levied until all bonds have been discharged and facilities funded and/or guaranteed, and the ONE TIME DEVELOPMENT TAX has been paid; however, the ANNUAL DEVELOPMENT TAX shall not be levied for a period in excess of twenty-five (25) years. COLLECTION OF ANNUAL TAX Residential: FIRST, determine monies necessary to make payment on bonds, debt service, replenish reserve and pay for public capital facilities. SECOND, levy and determine amount of funds available from special residential development tax. THIRD, to the extent monies are necessary, collect the remainder from the annual special tax rate per developable area for undeveloped land. Commercial and Industrial: FIRST, determine monies necessary to make payment on bonds, debt service, replenish reserve and pay for public capital facilities. SECOND, determine monies available from the (a) One Time Special Development Tax and (b) maximum collected Annual Special Development Tax. THIRD, to the extent monies are necessary, levy taxes on undeveloped taxable commercial and industrial acreage, not to exceed the maximum tax.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Based upon the facts set forth below, does Councilmember John Mamaux have a financial interest which would preclude him under the provisions of Government Code § 87100 from participating in the decision by the City Council of the City of Carlsbad to form a citywide community facilities district under the provisions of the Mello-Roos Community Facilities Act of 1982? In 1986 the City Council of the City of Carlsbad adopted Chapter 21.90 of the City's Municipal Code establishing a growth management plan for the City. A copy of Chapter 21.90 is attached for your reference. Chapter 21.90 required the preparation of a Citywide Facilities and Improvements Plan which was adopted by the City Council in 1986 ("Citywide Plan"). The Citywide plan established numerous geographical zones within the City and required the establishment of plans for each zone (collectively, the "Local Plans"). The Citywide Plan and Local Plans create standards for the financing and construction of a wide variety of public facilities including parks, roads, freeway intersections, libraries, fire stations, civic center and other public facilities. These Plans have been adopted by the City Council. The Plans identify establishment of a community facilities district as one method for financing the public facilities and services identified in the various Plans. Section 21.90.130 provides that no development permit shall be approved unless such a permit is consistent with the Local Facilities Management Plan and unless provisions for all facilities and improvements related to the development project are provided or funded in a manner acceptable to the City Council. In order to guarantee the provision and funding of certain facilities which are required prior to the issuance of development permits within the City, the formation of a community facilities district has been proposed. The community facilities district, if formed, would authorize the issuance of approximately $117,000,000 in special tax bonds, which would be secured by the levy of special taxes against undeveloped property within the City of Carlsbad. The bonds would be utilized to finance all or a portion of a library, City hall facility, regional park, local roads, freeway interchanges, and a utility maintenance warehouse, as well as incidental expenditures and costs of issuance for the bonds. The City has previously determined that the construction or guarantee of the financing of the construction of these facilities is a necessary prerequisite under Chapter 21.90 and the Citywide Plan and various Local Plans to the issuance of development permits. As mentioned above, the special tax would be levied at least initially only against currently undeveloped property. For property designated for residential development, an annual special tax would be levied for each developable acre until the issuance of a building permit for that property. At the time of issuance of a building permit, such residential property would be subject to a one time special development tax. Following payment of the special development tax, the residential property would not be subject to the levy of further special taxes for this community facilities district, although the property might be subject to facilities fees established pursuant to Section 21.90.050 of Chapter 21.90. Prior to development, property designated for commercial and industrial development would pay an annual special tax per developable acre. Upon the issuance of a building permit for the development of such property, the owners of such commercial or industrial property would have the option of paying a one time development tax or assuming an annual special development tax based upon the square footage of the building area. A copy of the proposed special tax formula is attached hereto. In November, 1989, Councilmember Mamaux became employed by Center Mortgage, a lending institution, the principal office of which is located in the City of Carlsbad. The primary focus of the lending activities of Center Mortgage is to provide financing for $300,000 to $700,000 commercial and industrial developments. Center Mortgage is owned by C&N Bank Corp., a California corporation the sole shareholder which is Dean Greenberg. Mr. Greenberg is the owner of Center Development, a land development company located in the City of Carlsbad which is the owner of 69 parcels which include approximately 129.66 net taxable acres designated for commercial or industrial use within Local Facilities Management Zone 5 and 1 parcel which includes approximately 95.8 net taxable acres within Local Facilities Management Zone 25 of the City. Zone 5 contains a total of 207 taxable parcels which include 910 taxable acres owned by 40 different property owners. Zone 25 contains a total of 5 taxable parcels which include 191.52 taxable acres owned by 5 different property owners. The property owned by Center Development would be subject to the levy of special taxes if the community facilities district is established. The Center Development property will also receive the same benefit received by other undeveloped properties throughout the City from the establishment of the community facilities district, the levy of special taxes and the issuance of bonds, ie., the satisfaction of the condition precedent to the issuance of development permits by the establishment of a method for guaranteeing the financing of the construction of public facilities as required by Chapter 21.90. The total number of taxable acres within the City, which may be subject to the levy of special taxes if the community facilities district is established, is 7393 acres which are located in Zones 4 through 25, inclusive. The total number of parcels throughout these zones is 1,451 and these parcels are owned by 496 owners. Zones 1 through 3 are not proposed to be included in the community facilities district because these zones have guaranteed the provision and funding of the facilities required for these zones through other methods. CITY OF CARLSBAD COMMUNITY FACILITIES DISTRICT NO. 1 EXHIBIT "A" PROPERTY CATEGORIES AND MAXIMUM TAX All taxable property within the boundaries of the Community Facilities District shall be subject to ANNUAL TAXES and/or DEVELOPMENT TAXES as applicable, based upon the property categories, maximum tax rates, increases and status of the issuance of building permits, all as hereinafter set forth. Area I refers to all properties within the boundaries of the City of Carlsbad Bridge and Thoroughfare District. Area II is for all remaining properties within the Community Facilities District, not within the Bridge & Thoroughfare District. RESIDENTIAL PROPERTY ANNUAL TAX All taxable property prior to the issuance of a building permit shall be subject to the following maximum annual special taxes, also subject to adjustments as set forth herein: MAXIMUM ANNUAL SPECIAL TAX RATE PER DEVELOPABLE ACRE FOR FISCAL YEAR 1990/91

embedding.first_500_words: April 17, 1990 Deanne Stone Kathy Donovan Fair Political Practices Commission Enforcement Division P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, California 95804 RE: REQUEST FOR FPPC OPINION - COUNCIL MEMBER MAMAUX'S PARTICIPATION IN DECISIONS REGARDING ESTABLISHMENT OF A CITY-WIDE COMMUNITY FACILITIES DISTRICT Ladies: This is in response to a letter dated April 3, 1990 from Tom Smith. The City contemplates the issuance of $117 million of "Mello-Roos" bonds to finance facilities required by the City's voter approved, award-winning growth management program. Tom Smith has consistently opposed growth management. See Concerned Citizens v. Carlsbad, (1988) 204 Cal.App.3d 937. The only interest bond counsel or I have in this matter is to insure the integrity of the bond issue. Bond counsel will be issuing a legal opinion in that regard. The absurdity of Smith's suggestion that bond counsel or I would compromise ourselves or the bond issue by giving you anything else but the true and correct facts in our March 21, 1990 request for advice speaks for itself. We are used to Smith's attacks on people's character which characterize his approach to politics and they require no reply. F. MacKenzie Brown has acted as the City's bond counsel for assessment district proceedings in Carlsbad for over 18 years. One of Smith's complaints involves Mr. Mamaux's participation in the Palomar Airport Road Assessment District. As previously reported bond counsel has given an opinion on that which does not relate in any way to the request for advice we have sent you on the proposed city-wide Community Facilities District. I have sent a copy of Smith's letter to bond counsel with a request that, to the extent any reply is necessary for a full disclosure of the facts, they communicate directly with you. I have also sent a copy to Mr. Mamaux in case he thinks it is necessary to comment. The image you provided is mostly blank, with very faint and mostly illegible text in the upper left corner. The majority of the page appears to be white or very light-colored, with no significant content or readable information aside from some faint markings that are not fully visible or clear enough to transcribe accurately. If there is any specific detail or question related to this image, please provide more context or another view for better results. We object to Smith's suggestion that the City's request for advice be delayed pending a resolution of his complaints against Mr. Mamaux. There is no rational connection. Staff believes the Community Facilities District is necessary to finance the public facilities required to preserve the quality of life in Carlsbad. They intend to submit the first step in the formation process, a resolution of intention, to the Council in the next few weeks. We ask that you not encourage Mr. Smith in his efforts to oppose that by responding to his request for a delay. A prompt response to our request for advice and the resolution of all of the pending complaints against Mr. Mamaux as soon as possible will
